business_core: ABB India manufactures and sells industrial automation, electrification, motion control and robotics products and systems — the equipment and software that runs factories, power grids, data centres and infrastructure projects. It operates as the Indian listed subsidiary of Swiss-headquartered ABB Group, combining global engineering technology with local manufacturing and service.
moat_note: Decades of ABB Group's global engineering technology, brand trust with large industrial and utility customers, and an integrated local ecosystem of manufacturing, service and support that new entrants cannot replicate quickly, even as competitive intensity from Chinese suppliers grows.
name: ABB India
source_note: ABB India Q4/CY25 results coverage (ICICI Direct, Motilal Oswal, Upstox, Whalesbook, Samco); Screener.in
value_chain_note: As a majority-owned subsidiary of Swiss-headquartered ABB Group, its product roadmap, technology transfer and even strategic decisions about specific business lines (like the recent robotics divestment question) flow from group-level decisions made outside India.
value_chain_position: ABB India sits in the middle of the industrial value chain as a technology and equipment supplier — it manufactures and sells automation, electrification and motion products and systems to industrial, infrastructure, utility and data-centre customers, who then use that equipment to run their own factories, grids and facilities.

business_core: Adani Energy Solutions builds and operates high-voltage power transmission lines and distributes electricity directly to consumers, primarily serving metropolitan Mumbai and the Mundra Special Economic Zone through subsidiary Adani Electricity Mumbai. As India's largest private transmission company, it earns revenue from regulated transmission tariffs and direct electricity distribution and metering to over 13 million consumers.
moat_note: India's largest private transmission company, with an extensive high-voltage network (nearly 28,000 circuit kilometres) and regulatory licenses that are difficult and slow for new entrants to obtain, reinforced by its position within the broader Adani energy ecosystem.
name: Adani Energy Solutions
source_note: Adani Energy Solutions Q4 FY26 results coverage (MarketsMojo); Screener.in; company investor presentation
value_chain_note: AESL sits at the exact chokepoint that determines whether renewable power actually reaches the market: its own group disclosures acknowledge that Adani Green's FY26 curtailment losses were partly a function of how fast AESL's own transmission build-out could keep pace — a direct, quantifiable link between these two related companies.
value_chain_position: Adani Energy Solutions builds and operates the transmission lines and distribution networks that carry electricity from generators — including renewable plants like sister company Adani Green Energy — to end consumers, plus directly distributes power to over 13 million consumers in Mumbai and the Mundra SEZ.

as_of: Q4 FY26 (quarter ended 31 Mar 2026); FY26 for EBITDA mix and PAT
business_core: Adani Enterprises is the flagship 'incubator' company of the Adani Group — it builds and scales new infrastructure businesses (airports, roads, data centers, green hydrogen/new energy, copper smelting, mining services) from greenfield stage to maturity, then either keeps them as core holdings or spins them off into separately listed entities (as it has done historically with Adani Ports, Adani Green and Adani Power, and this year with its Adani Wilmar stake). Because it's perpetually building new capital-intensive assets, reported quarterly profit is often distorted by depreciation on newly commissioned projects and one-off divestment gains — the underlying operating EBITDA trend is a more useful signal than any single quarter's headline PAT.
moat_note: A genuinely distinctive 'business incubation' model within Indian conglomerates — the demonstrated ability to build, fund and scale multiple large infrastructure businesses from greenfield to maturity and eventually spin them off as independently valuable listed entities is a real, differentiated capability few Indian corporate groups have replicated at this scale. The moat's dependence on continued access to large-scale capital at reasonable cost is also its key vulnerability, tightly linked to the broader group's governance reputation.
name: Adani Enterprises
source_note: Adani Enterprises' own Q4 & FY26 results release (adani.com), cross-checked against Republic World, Outlook Business and Business Today
value_chain_note: This is a genuinely different value-creation mechanism than a typical single-business operating company — investors are effectively buying exposure to the Adani Group's next generation of infrastructure businesses before they're mature enough to stand alone.
value_chain_position: Operates a distinctive 'build-mature-monetise' model — incubating capital-intensive infrastructure businesses from scratch (funded by group capital and debt), scaling them to commercial maturity, and then either retaining the cash flows or spinning them off as separately listed companies once self-sustaining — the model that created Adani Ports, Adani Green, Adani Power and Adani Energy Solutions as independent entities.

business_core: Adani Green Energy builds, owns and operates utility-scale solar, wind and hybrid power plants across India, then sells the electricity onward under long-term Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) to state distribution companies (DISCOMs), the government's Solar Energy Corporation of India (SECI), and increasingly to commercial & industrial (C&I) customers directly. It earns a spread between what it costs to generate power (largely fixed, once a plant is built) and what buyers pay under multi-decade contracts. It is India's largest renewable energy company by operating capacity.
moat_note: Scale, balance-sheet access, and the Khavda site itself — the world's largest single renewable energy park (30 GW planned) on barren land, a location and land-bank advantage that is extremely difficult for any competitor to replicate.
name: Adani Green Energy
source_note: Adani Green Energy FY26 results presentation & press release (adanigreenenergy.com); Screener.in; MarketsMojo Q4 FY26 result analysis; SEBI Reg 31(4) promoter declaration coverage (scanx.trade)
value_chain_note: Because a sister Adani company (Adani Energy Solutions) builds much of the transmission infrastructure that carries this power to market, AGEL is unusually exposed to how fast that related company's grid build-out keeps pace — the FY26 curtailment losses are a direct, measurable example of that dependency.
value_chain_position: An upstream power generator — Adani Green builds and owns utility-scale solar, wind and hybrid plants, then sells the electricity onward under long-term contracts. It sits between land/equipment suppliers and the buyers (DISCOMs, SECI, and increasingly C&I customers) who consume the power.

business_core: Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone (APSEZ) is India's largest private port operator, running 12+ ports across India's coastline (Mundra being the flagship) plus a growing international portfolio (Australia's NQXT, Colombo's CWIT, Haifa in Israel). It's deliberately evolving from a pure cargo-handling port operator into an integrated logistics and marine (offshore support vessel) platform, capturing more of the value chain around each container or tonne of cargo.
moat_note: Scale (India's largest private port operator, ~27-28% market share) combined with strategic port locations (Mundra especially) and an increasingly integrated ports-to-logistics-to-marine platform create a genuine infrastructure moat that's expensive and slow for competitors to replicate — reinforced by the ports-privatisation policy tailwind that continues to favour established, well-capitalised operators.
name: Adani Ports and Special Economic Zone
source_note: APSEZ's own Q4 & FY26 results release, cross-checked against ITLN, Newkerala and Dailyhunt coverage
value_chain_note: India's port-privatisation policy tailwind continues to favour established, well-capitalised operators like APSEZ for new concessions, reinforcing scale advantages already built over two decades.
value_chain_position: Sits at the critical trade-infrastructure chokepoint between global shipping and India's domestic economy — its ports handle roughly 27-28% of India's total port cargo — and it's deliberately extending both backward (marine/offshore vessel services) and forward (logistics, trucking, warehousing, rail) to capture more of the value chain around each tonne of cargo.

business_core: Adani Power generates electricity primarily from coal-fired thermal plants — led by the 4,620 MW Mundra facility — and sells it under a mix of long-term Power Purchase Agreements with state utilities, shorter-term contracts, and open merchant-market sales. As India's largest private thermal power producer, it plays a central role in the country's baseload electricity supply even as the broader system adds renewable capacity.
moat_note: Scale as India's largest private thermal power producer, accounting for roughly 7.3% of India's domestic coal-based power generation capacity, backed by the Adani Group's execution track record and access to captive and contracted coal supply.
name: Adani Power
source_note: Adani Power FY26 & Q4 FY26 results release (adani.com); Screener.in; Kotak Neo shareholding data
value_chain_note: Because a meaningful share of its output is sold on the merchant market rather than locked into long-term contracts, Adani Power's earnings are directly exposed to day-ahead power price swings — exactly the kind of swing that cut average realisations 13.7% in FY26 as renewable penetration rose.
value_chain_position: Adani Power burns coal to generate baseload electricity at large thermal plants (led by the 4,620 MW Mundra facility), then sells that power through a mix of long-term Power Purchase Agreements with state utilities, shorter-term contracts, and open merchant-market sales — a classic thermal generator business model.

business_core: Ambuja Cements manufactures, markets and sells cement and ready-mix concrete to home builders, developers, institutional clients, masons, contractors and construction professionals across India. Part of the Adani Group's cement platform (which also includes ACC, Sanghi and Penna Cement, recently consolidated under a 'One Cement Platform' strategy), it is one of India's largest cement producers by volume.
moat_note: Scale as part of the Adani Group's consolidated cement platform (Ambuja, ACC, Sanghi, Penna), a debt-free balance sheet with the highest credit ratings in the sector, and an extensive pan-India plant and distribution network built over decades.
name: Ambuja Cements
source_note: Ambuja Cements FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Upstox, Tribune India, ScanX earnings call, TradeBrains); Screener.in
value_chain_note: As part of Adani Group's cement consolidation strategy (Ambuja, ACC, Sanghi, Penna, Orient all being merged under one platform), Ambuja's value chain now includes integrating recently acquired plants whose cost structures and regional demand exposure (like Penna's South India concentration) differ meaningfully from its legacy assets.
value_chain_position: Ambuja Cements mines limestone and other raw materials, manufactures cement and clinker at integrated plants and grinding units across India, and sells finished cement and ready-mix concrete to builders, contractors and institutional construction customers — a vertically integrated cement manufacturer within the broader Adani Group materials portfolio.

business_core: Apollo Hospitals is India's largest private healthcare provider, running a network of hospitals (its core, most profitable 'Healthcare Services' segment) alongside a large omni-channel pharmacy and digital health business (Apollo HealthCo, including Apollo 24/7) and a retail diagnostics/healthcare arm (AHLL) — a genuinely integrated healthcare ecosystem beyond just hospital beds, with a planned demerger of the pharmacy/digital arm into a separately listed entity targeted by Q4 FY27.
moat_note: India's largest, most recognized private hospital brand plus a genuinely integrated ecosystem (hospitals + pharmacy + diagnostics + digital) that few competitors replicate at this scale — reinforced by high-end specialty capabilities (oncology, cardiac, transplants, robotics) that are expensive and slow for regional competitors to build from scratch.
name: Apollo Hospitals
source_note: Apollo Hospitals' own Q4 & FY26 results, cross-checked against BW Healthcare World, Business Today and MarketsMojo coverage
value_chain_note: International/medical-value-travel patients (from Africa, the Middle East, Bangladesh and Southeast Asia) provide an additional, typically higher-realization revenue stream leveraging India's cost and clinical-quality advantage in specialties like oncology, transplants and robotic surgery.
value_chain_position: Spans the healthcare value chain from hospital-based tertiary/quaternary care (the core, most profitable segment) through omni-channel pharmacy distribution (Apollo HealthCo, ~7,289 stores) to digital health and diagnostics (Apollo 24/7, AHLL) — a genuinely integrated ecosystem most single-format hospital chains or pure-play pharmacy retailers don't have.

business_core: Asian Paints is India's largest paint company — decorative paints (interior/exterior wall paints) make up roughly 90% of revenue, sold through India's largest paint dealer network, alongside a smaller industrial coatings business, international operations, and a growing 'Beautiful Homes Store' push into home décor and renovation services to capture more of a customer's overall home-improvement spend.
moat_note: Decades of brand trust and India's largest paint dealer/distribution network form a genuine, historically hard-to-replicate reach moat. That moat is being tested for the first time in years by a well-capitalised, Aditya Birla-backed competitor (Birla Opus) with clear ambitions and early, real market-share gains — worth watching whether Asian Paints' distribution advantage holds up against a rival with comparable group-level financial backing.
name: Asian Paints
source_note: Asian Paints' own Q4 & FY26 results release, cross-checked against Business Standard, Storyboard18 and Upstox
value_chain_note: Decorative paints (~90% of revenue) means the business is heavily tied to India's residential real estate and repainting cycle — a slowdown in premium/luxury housing affordability could directly reduce repainting frequency and new-construction demand.
value_chain_position: Sits at the finished-goods end of the coatings value chain — sources crude-linked raw materials (titanium dioxide, resins, solvents) globally and converts them into branded paint sold through India's largest paint dealer network, while extending into adjacent home-improvement services (Beautiful Homes Stores) to capture more of a customer's total renovation spend beyond just the paint can.

business_core: AU Small Finance Bank converted from a vehicle-finance NBFC into a small finance bank in 2017. It lends heavily to underbanked retail and MSME customers in semi-urban and rural Rajasthan and neighbouring states — vehicle and MSME loans originally, now alongside a fuller banking suite of deposits and cards.
moat_note: A digital-first, tech-forward small finance bank with the broadest branch network among SFBs (2,700+ touchpoints across 21 states) + a fresh in-principle approval to become a Universal Bank, a structural upgrade most SFB peers don't yet have in hand.
name: AU Small Finance Bank
source_note: AU Small Finance Bank's own Q3 FY26 results and corroborating market coverage
value_chain_position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. AU's specific exposure is the small-finance-bank sector's microfinance stress plus an intense deposit-competition environment — set against a structural upgrade, an in-principle RBI approval to become a Universal Bank.

business_core: Axis Bank is India's third-largest private bank: it takes deposits and lends across retail, SME and corporate banking, earning the interest spread plus fee income from cards, transaction banking and third-party product distribution (insurance, mutual funds) sold to its customers.
moat_note: Scale as India's third-largest private bank + a strong digital/UPI franchise (~36-39% market share in UPI Payer PSP volume) + a diversified retail/SME/corporate mix that spreads concentration risk.
name: Axis Bank
source_note: Axis Bank's own Q3 FY26 results announcement (axis.bank.in) and corroborating market coverage
value_chain_position: No physical chain, but Axis sits unusually close to the payments layer itself — it is one of the largest UPI payer-PSP (sponsor) banks by volume, routing a big share of UPI payer-side transactions. That is strategically central to the rails, yet revenue-light under zero-MDR.

business_core: Bajaj Auto is India's largest exporter of two/three-wheelers and a top-3 domestic two-wheeler maker (Pulsar, Platina, Chetak electric scooter), with KTM and Triumph premium-motorcycle partnerships/stakes. It manufactures and sells motorcycles, scooters and three-wheelers domestically and to over 70 export markets (notably Africa, Latin America, ASEAN), plus a fast-growing electric-vehicle portfolio.
moat_note: Brand strength across price points (mass-market Pulsar/Platina to premium KTM/Triumph) plus the widest export distribution network of any Indian two-wheeler maker — a scale and reach moat few domestic rivals can match.
name: Bajaj Auto
source_note: Bajaj Auto's own Q3 FY26 press release (bajajauto.com), JM Financial, Business Standard, and ICICI Direct — cross-checked
value_chain_note: KTM AG (Austria), in which Bajaj holds a significant stake, went through public financial distress in 2024-25 — Bajaj has been infusing capital into its subsidiaries partly to support this relationship, a direct real-time link between European industrial trouble and an Indian company's balance sheet.
value_chain_position: Bajaj assembles two/three-wheelers domestically (Pune/Chakan/Aurangabad) and exports nearly half its volumes — giving it direct, quarter-by-quarter exposure to emerging-market currencies (especially the Nigerian naira and other African currencies) that a purely domestic-focused peer wouldn't carry. Its KTM (Austria) and Triumph (UK) partnerships/stakes also plug it into European motorcycle-brand value chains.

as_of: Q4 FY26 (quarter ended 31 Mar 2026); FY26 for full-year figures
business_core: Bajaj Finserv is the holding company of the Bajaj group's financial-services businesses — its value comes almost entirely from three large subsidiaries: Bajaj Finance (India's leading consumer-lending NBFC), Bajaj Allianz General Insurance and Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance (now renamed after Bajaj bought out Allianz's stake). Owning Bajaj Finserv is effectively owning a bundle of lending plus general and life insurance through one listed vehicle, with a smaller set of emerging businesses (health, digital, asset management) still in investment/loss-making mode.
moat_note: A rare, genuinely diversified financial-services platform under one roof — a market-leading consumer-lending NBFC (Bajaj Finance) plus now-wholly-owned general and life insurers — combined with a strong brand and one of India's widest financial-product distribution reaches. The moat is real, but the holding-company structure means investors get diluted, indirect exposure to each business, and this year's insurance weakness shows the diversification cuts both ways: no single strong business fully offsets a weak one at the group level.
name: Bajaj Finserv
source_note: Bajaj Finserv's own Q4 & FY26 results and investor presentation, cross-checked against MarketsMojo, Whalesbook and APAC Media coverage
value_chain_note: The buyout of Allianz's stake marks a strategic pivot from a joint-venture model (shared control, shared risk) to full ownership (full control, full risk) — greater strategic flexibility, but also greater concentration of both upside and execution risk in Bajaj Finserv's own hands.
value_chain_position: Sits at the top of a diversified financial-services group as a holding company — it doesn't directly originate loans or underwrite policies itself, but captures the aggregate value of India's leading consumer-lending NBFC (Bajaj Finance) plus now-wholly-owned general and life insurers, giving investors bundled exposure to both credit-risk-bearing lending and insurance underwriting in one vehicle.

business_core: Bajaj Housing Finance is a housing finance company that lends to individuals for home purchase and construction, and to developers for project finance, as a subsidiary of Bajaj Finance. It borrows in wholesale debt markets and lends that capital onward at a margin, benefiting from its parent's brand, distribution network and underwriting capabilities.
moat_note: Structural competitive advantages inherited from parent Bajaj Finance — brand equity, distribution depth, underwriting technology and customer relationships — that would take a new entrant 10-15 years to replicate independently.
name: Bajaj Housing Finance
source_note: Bajaj Housing Finance Q4 FY26 results coverage (MarketsMojo, Univest); Screener.in
value_chain_note: As a subsidiary of Bajaj Finance, it can lean on the parent's distribution network, credit underwriting systems and funding relationships rather than building all of that from scratch, giving it a faster path to scale than an independent housing financier.
value_chain_position: Bajaj Housing Finance borrows money in the wholesale debt and capital markets, then lends it onward to individuals and developers for home purchases and construction — a classic housing-finance NBFC model, sitting between capital markets and the end borrower.

business_core: Bajaj Holdings & Investment Limited is a pure investment holding company with no significant operating business of its own. Its value comes almost entirely from large strategic equity stakes it holds in three Bajaj group companies — Bajaj Auto, Bajaj Finserv and Bajaj Finance — earning dividends and capital appreciation from those holdings rather than from manufacturing or selling anything directly.
moat_note: Multi-decade, deeply entrenched strategic stakes in three large, well-run Bajaj group companies — a position that would be effectively impossible for anyone to replicate today given how the ownership was built up over generations.
name: Bajaj Holdings
source_note: Bajaj Holdings Q4 FY26 results coverage (MarketsMojo)
value_chain_note: Because its own standalone operations are negligible, Bajaj Holdings' real 'business' is capital allocation and long-term ownership — its value chain position is best understood as sitting one layer above the operating companies themselves, rather than participating in any single industry's chain directly.
value_chain_position: Bajaj Holdings & Investment is a pure investment holding company — it doesn't manufacture or sell anything itself, but owns large strategic stakes in Bajaj Auto, Bajaj Finserv and Bajaj Finance, capturing value purely through dividends received and the appreciation of those underlying stakes.

business_core: Bajaj Finance is a non-deposit-taking NBFC — instead of collecting public deposits like a bank, it borrows wholesale from banks and bond markets and re-lends that money at a spread. Its signature business is point-of-sale consumer lending (EMI financing for electronics and appliances in-store), alongside personal loans, SME loans and mortgages.
moat_note: Scale + a 115 million+ customer franchise + an early, aggressive bet on embedding AI into underwriting and collections (its own 'FinAI' platform) — a distribution and data moat that's hard for smaller NBFCs to replicate.
name: Bajaj Finance
source_note: Bajaj Finance's own Q3 FY26 investor presentation (bajajfinserv.in) and corroborating market coverage
value_chain_position: No physical chain — an NBFC's value chain is funding cost versus lending yield. Bajaj borrows wholesale (it has no CASA deposits) and lends across consumer and SME, so it is structurally more rate- and funding-sensitive than a bank, and squarely inside the unsecured-consumer-credit segment RBI has been tightening.

business_core: Bandhan Bank grew out of India's largest microfinance institution, so a meaningful share of its book is still unsecured, group-liability microfinance lending to low-income borrowers — higher-yield but higher-risk — alongside a growing secured retail and housing-finance business it's been building to diversify.
moat_note: Deep, two-decade-old rural/semi-urban microfinance distribution and credit-assessment expertise — a genuine niche moat in a segment most universal banks avoid, though it carries structurally higher volatility through credit cycles than a diversified bank.
name: Bandhan Bank
source_note: Bandhan Bank's own Q3 FY26 press release and corroborating market coverage
value_chain_position: No physical chain, but Bandhan's model is built on microfinance (its EEB book), so it sits at the sharp end of the sector-wide MFI stress cycle — and its footprint is also geographically concentrated in East India.

business_core: Bank of Baroda is a government-owned (PSU) bank that takes deposits and lends across retail, agriculture, MSME and corporate India, with a meaningful overseas branch network left from its historically international footprint. Profitability is driven by its interest spread and continued recovery in asset quality after the sector-wide PSU-bank bad-loan cycle.
moat_note: Scale as India's second-largest PSU bank + an extensive pan-India branch network + government-backed trust — the same structural advantages as SBI, at a smaller scale, with a credibly improving balance sheet after years of post-merger cleanup.
name: Bank of Baroda
source_note: Bank of Baroda's own Q3 FY26 results and corroborating market coverage
value_chain_position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. As a large government-owned bank, Bank of Baroda carries state policy priorities alongside commercial goals, and emerged bigger from India's PSU-bank consolidation (the Vijaya and Dena merger).

as_of: Q4 FY26 (quarter ended 31 Mar 2026); FY26 for revenue/PAT growth and order book
business_core: Bharat Electronics (BEL) is India's leading state-owned (Navratna PSU) defence electronics company, designing and manufacturing radar systems, avionics, electronic warfare equipment and communication systems almost exclusively for the Indian armed forces, with a growing export component. It's a direct beneficiary of India's 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' defence-indigenisation push, which favours domestic suppliers like BEL over imports.
moat_note: A uniquely protected position as India's leading state-owned defence electronics manufacturer — decades of relationships with the Indian armed forces, security clearances and technology capabilities competitors (especially foreign ones) can't easily replicate, reinforced by explicit government policy favouring domestic defence suppliers. This is about as close to a government-guaranteed moat as exists on this dashboard, though BEL's growth is also somewhat capped by defence budget allocation decisions outside its own control.
name: Bharat Electronics
source_note: Bharat Electronics' own Q4 & FY26 results, cross-checked against Groww, PSU Connect and ChartAlert coverage
value_chain_note: That same government-customer concentration means payment timing and receivables are shaped more by government budget cycles than typical commercial terms — a structural feature of the business model, not a temporary issue.
value_chain_position: Sits at the design-and-manufacture end of India's defence electronics value chain, selling almost exclusively to the Indian Ministry of Defence and armed forces (with export sales a smaller but growing share) — a uniquely captive, policy-protected customer relationship commercial electronics manufacturers don't have.

business_core: Bharti Airtel sells mobile, home broadband and enterprise connectivity in India and 14 African countries, plus a growing digital/data-centre business. Revenue is billed per subscriber per month (ARPU) — so the whole thesis rests on getting more subscribers to pay more each month, in a market where a price war a few years ago wiped out a competitor (Vodafone Idea) and left effectively two strong players, Airtel and Reliance Jio.
moat_note: Spectrum licenses + a nationwide tower/fibre network are a real, expensive-to-replicate physical moat, reinforced by a rational two-player India pricing structure post-Vodafone Idea's decline. The open question is whether Jio's faster subscriber-acquisition pace (via FWA broadband) erodes Airtel's premium-subscriber advantage over time.
name: Bharti Airtel
source_note: Bharti Airtel's own Q4 FY26 BSE filing/investor release (bseindia.com) and earnings-call highlights, cross-checked against multiple financial media reports
value_chain_note: Effectively a two-player India wireless market after Vodafone Idea's collapse in relevance — pricing discipline between Airtel and Jio (rather than aggressive share wars) has structurally supported ARPU premiumisation industry-wide.
value_chain_position: Airtel sits as an infrastructure-owning network operator — it holds spectrum (licensed from the government, a major cost and regulatory dependency), builds towers/fibre, and sells connectivity directly to consumers and enterprises, with Africa run as a largely separate operating unit under its own listed subsidiary economics.

business_core: Bosch India manufactures and supplies technology and components across four sectors: Mobility (automotive parts like fuel injection and chassis systems), Industrial Technology, Consumer Goods (power tools and appliances), and Energy and Building Technology. As the Indian flagship of Germany's Robert Bosch group, it combines global engineering technology with extensive local manufacturing, supplying both automotive OEMs and industrial/retail customers directly.
moat_note: Deep engineering technology transfer from Robert Bosch GmbH's global R&D, combined with nearly 18 manufacturing units and decades of relationships with India's major automotive and industrial OEMs — scale and technical trust that would take a new entrant decades to replicate.
name: Bosch
source_note: Bosch India Q4 FY26 results coverage (Bajaj Finserv, Univest); Screener.in; Bosch Limited shareholder information (bosch.in); Kotak Neo
value_chain_note: Because Bosch India sits inside a much larger German multinational group, its own corporate structure is periodically reshuffled (subsidiaries transferred in or spun out) as part of global Bosch strategy — a structural factor investors need to track separately from underlying business performance.
value_chain_position: Bosch India manufactures components, technology and systems — spanning automotive parts (fuel injection, chassis systems), industrial automation, consumer goods (power tools, appliances) and building technology — selling to OEMs like Hyundai and TVS Motor as well as directly to industrial and retail customers, positioning it upstream of the vehicles and products that use its technology.

business_core: Bharat Petroleum Corporation refines crude oil into petrol, diesel, LPG, aviation turbine fuel and other products, then sells them through its own retail network, alongside a smaller exploration & production business. As a government-controlled Maharatna PSU, it is one of India's three dominant oil marketing companies, with earnings driven mainly by refining margins (the spread between crude cost and refined product prices) and steady domestic fuel demand growth.
moat_note: A Maharatna PSU with an extensive, hard-to-replicate retail fuel network across India, integrated refining capacity, and government backing — scale and distribution reach that would take a private competitor decades and enormous capital to build from scratch.
name: Bharat Petroleum
source_note: BPCL FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Groww, PSU Connect, Upstox); Screener.in; BPCL Q4 FY26 earnings call transcript; ScanX SEBI filing coverage
value_chain_note: Because crude oil is priced globally in dollars while BPCL sells fuel domestically in rupees at government-influenced prices, the company sits at the exact point where global crude volatility and Indian currency and policy risk intersect — a structurally different exposure than a pure producer or a pure retailer would have.
value_chain_position: BPCL sits in the middle of the oil value chain — downstream of crude oil producers like ONGC, it refines crude into petrol, diesel, LPG and other fuels, then sells them onward through its own retail network, distinguishing it from purely upstream companies that only extract crude.

business_core: Britannia Industries manufactures and sells packaged food products — primarily biscuits, along with bread, dairy, cakes and adjacent categories — under well-known brands sold through extensive domestic retail and rural distribution networks as well as export markets in over 60 countries. It is one of India's oldest and most trusted FMCG companies, particularly dominant in the biscuits category.
moat_note: Decades of brand trust in Indian households across biscuits (NutriChoice, Bourbon, Good Day) and dairy, combined with one of the deepest rural distribution networks in Indian FMCG — advantages that support pricing power new entrants cannot easily replicate.
name: Britannia Industries
source_note: Britannia FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (MarketsMojo, ChartAlert, ScanX, Univest); Screener.in
value_chain_note: Because its core inputs are agricultural commodities with volatile global pricing, Britannia's margin story each quarter is as much about input cost timing and hedging as it is about brand strength or volume growth — a structurally different risk profile from a services or technology business.
value_chain_position: Britannia converts commodity agricultural inputs — wheat flour, palm oil, sugar and dairy — into branded, packaged biscuits, bread, dairy and adjacent food products, sold through India's vast retail and rural distribution networks as well as 60+ export markets.

business_core: Canara Bank is a PSU bank with a large branch network concentrated in South India, taking deposits and lending across retail, agriculture, MSME and corporate customers. Like other PSU banks, its profitability is tied to the interest spread and to continued recovery in legacy asset quality.
moat_note: Scale as India's fourth-largest PSU bank and sixth-largest bank overall (10,000+ domestic branches), ranked #4 as lead bank across India's states/UTs/districts — deep, sovereign-anchored distribution that's nearly impossible for a private bank to replicate quickly.
name: Canara Bank
source_note: Canara Bank's own Q3 FY26 results, Screener.in's ratio summary, and India Ratings commentary
value_chain_position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. Government-owned, and one of India's largest PSU banks with a lead-bank role coordinating banking across many states and districts — deep, sovereign-anchored distribution.

business_core: CG Power and Industrial Solutions manufactures power equipment (transformers, switchgears), industrial motors, and rail transportation systems, and has recently expanded into semiconductor assembly and testing through its CG Semi subsidiary. Part of the Murugappa Group (via Tube Investments of India), it is a major Indian electrical equipment manufacturer undergoing rapid capacity expansion.
moat_note: Established brand trust and manufacturing scale in power equipment built over decades under the Murugappa Group umbrella, now reinforced by an early, capital-backed entry into semiconductor assembly and testing — a genuinely differentiated diversification most power equipment peers lack.
name: CG Power and Industrial Solutions
source_note: CG Power FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Power Peak Digest, Tickertape); Screener.in
value_chain_note: The move into semiconductor assembly and testing (via CG Semi) is a genuine strategic diversification beyond CG Power's traditional power equipment business, positioning it to benefit from India's government-backed semiconductor manufacturing push — a structurally different growth vector than its legacy transformer and motor businesses.
value_chain_position: CG Power manufactures transformers, switchgears and industrial motors sold to utilities, industrial customers and infrastructure developers, plus rail transportation equipment, and has now added semiconductor assembly and testing services — positioning it both as a traditional power equipment supplier and an emerging participant in India's electronics manufacturing supply chain.

business_core: Cholamandalam Investment & Finance (Murugappa Group) is an NBFC built mainly around vehicle finance — commercial vehicles, cars and tractors — plus home loans and SME lending. Like Bajaj Finance, it borrows wholesale and lends at a spread, so its profit is driven by loan-book growth, asset quality through vehicle-financing cycles, and its own cost of borrowing.
moat_note: A decades-old vehicle-finance distribution network (1,600+ branches) under the Murugappa Group umbrella, now diversifying into home loans, SME and gold loans to reduce reliance on any single credit cycle — though vehicle finance concentration is still its biggest single swing factor.
name: Cholamandalam Investment & Finance
source_note: Cholamandalam's own Q3 FY26 press release (murugappa.com) and corroborating market/analyst coverage
value_chain_position: No physical chain — funding cost versus lending yield. Chola is anchored in vehicle finance, so it rides the commercial-vehicle and auto cycle, with a large rural and semi-urban customer base sensitive to the monsoon and rural income.

business_core: Cipla is India's third-largest pharmaceutical company, selling respiratory, anti-diabetes, cardiac and urology branded generics domestically (its 'One-India' business) alongside a US generics/specialty business and a growing Africa/emerging-markets presence. Its US business, historically a key profit driver, is currently going through a rough patch as several high-margin legacy products lose their earning power at the same time.
moat_note: A genuinely strong, market-leading India respiratory franchise (Cipla is historically India's #1 respiratory brand) plus a broad emerging-markets footprint provide real, durable moats. The US specialty-generics business, historically a high-margin growth driver, has just demonstrated real fragility to single-product and single-facility concentration risk — a moat weakness this quarter exposed rather than created, and one management is now directly addressing through US-based manufacturing.
name: Cipla
source_note: Cipla's own Q4 & FY26 press release (cipla.com) and results filing, cross-checked against Business Standard, Whalesbook and ICICI Direct Research
value_chain_note: The Fall River facility and a newly approved first AB-rated generic Ventolin from a US site are direct, concrete responses to the single-facility fragility this quarter's results just exposed — not a hypothetical mitigation plan.
value_chain_position: A vertically-oriented generics and specialty pharma manufacturer with meaningful India-based manufacturing (multiple USFDA-inspected facilities: Bommasandra, Sitec, Medispray) plus a newly operational US manufacturing facility (Fall River) aimed directly at reducing the geographic/regulatory concentration risk that contributed to this quarter's Lanreotide disruption.

business_core: Coal India is the world's largest coal-mining company — a state-owned (Maharatna) PSU that mines roughly 80% of India's domestic coal and supplies it to power plants and industry, mostly through long-term Fuel Supply Agreements at government-notified prices, plus a smaller, more profitable e-auction channel at market-linked premium pricing. Revenue is fundamentally a function of production volume, offtake (how much actually gets sold/dispatched) and per-tonne realization — not open commodity trading.
moat_note: A state-granted, near-monopoly position as India's dominant domestic coal supplier — decades of mining rights, Fuel Supply Agreement relationships with power utilities, and evacuation infrastructure that would be near-impossible for a private entrant to replicate. The same government relationship that creates this moat also caps CIL's ability to behave like a pure profit-maximising commodity producer — pricing and dividend decisions carry a policy dimension private miners don't have.
name: Coal India
source_note: Coal India's own Q4 & FY26 consolidated results and production/offtake filings, cross-checked against Business Standard, Upstox and IndianPSU coverage
value_chain_note: As a Maharatna PSU under the Ministry of Coal, capital allocation (dividend policy especially) is shaped as much by government fiscal priorities as by pure commercial logic — a structural feature of the stock, not a temporary condition.
value_chain_position: Sits at the pure extraction end of the energy value chain, mining coal across 310 mines in 8 states and delivering it mainly through long-term Fuel Supply Agreements to power utilities and industrial buyers at largely government-notified prices — with a smaller e-auction channel offering the more market-sensitive, higher-margin slice of volume.

business_core: Divi's Laboratories manufactures active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs), pharmaceutical intermediates and nutraceutical ingredients (carotenoids, vitamins) for global pharmaceutical and nutraceutical companies. It operates as a specialist, behind-the-scenes supplier rather than a branded drug maker, earning revenue through generic API sales, custom synthesis contract manufacturing for Big Pharma, and nutraceutical ingredient supply.
moat_note: World's largest manufacturer for 10 major generic APIs, combined with decade-plus relationships with 12 of the top 20 global Big Pharma companies — regulatory trust, quality consistency and manufacturing complexity that took decades to build and cannot be replicated quickly.
name: Divi's Laboratories
source_note: Divi's Laboratories FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Zerodha/Reuters, Alpha Spread investor relations); Screener.in
value_chain_note: Because Divi's manufactures the building blocks rather than the final medicine, its fortunes are tied to global pharma companies' sourcing and pricing decisions — both a strength (deep, long-term customer relationships with Big Pharma) and a structural exposure (generic API pricing pressure that finished-drug companies can pass upstream).
value_chain_position: Divi's Laboratories sits upstream in the pharmaceutical value chain as a specialist manufacturer — it makes the active pharmaceutical ingredients (APIs) and intermediates that global pharmaceutical companies then formulate into finished, branded or generic medicines, rather than selling any drugs under its own brand.

business_core: DLF develops and sells residential real estate — from mass housing to super-luxury towers — and separately leases commercial office space, retail malls and hospitality assets for recurring rental income. It earns large, lumpy cash inflows when residential projects are sold and completed, plus steadier annuity income from long-term leases, and is India's largest private real estate developer by scale and land bank.
moat_note: A multi-decade land bank across Gurugram and other NCR micro-markets, built up since the 1970s, combined with a premium brand (DLF Camellias, Privana, Dahlias) that commands pricing power new entrants cannot easily replicate.
name: DLF
source_note: DLF FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Zeebiz, InvestyWise); Screener.in; Kotak Neo & IndiaInfoline shareholding/market cap data
value_chain_note: Unlike an industrial company, DLF's core 'input' is land itself — its decades-accumulated land bank in Gurugram and other NCR locations is arguably a bigger competitive asset than any single project, since new entrants cannot easily replicate a multi-decade land position.
value_chain_position: A real estate developer sitting between land and the end buyer or tenant — DLF acquires land, builds residential and commercial projects, then either sells units outright (development business) or leases office and retail space for recurring rental income (annuity business).

business_core: Avenue Supermarts operates the DMart chain of value-format supermarkets across India, selling food, non-food FMCG and general merchandise & apparel under an 'Everyday Low Cost - Everyday Low Price' strategy built on owned real estate, bulk procurement and operational efficiency. It is India's most profitable large-format organised retailer, though it also operates DMart Ready, a still-developing e-commerce and quick-commerce-adjacent channel.
moat_note: Industry-leading store-level economics from owned (not leased) real estate and disciplined bulk procurement, built over two decades and extremely difficult for a new entrant to replicate at DMart's scale and cost structure.
name: Avenue Supermarts (DMart)
source_note: Avenue Supermarts (DMart) FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Angel One, ScanX, Univest, Multibagg AI); Screener.in
value_chain_note: DMart's traditional moat — owned real estate and bulk procurement driving the lowest cost-per-unit in Indian retail — is now being tested by quick commerce players who compete on convenience and speed rather than price, forcing DMart to build out DMart Ready even though that channel doesn't yet match its physical stores' economics.
value_chain_position: Avenue Supermarts operates the DMart chain of large-format, owned-real-estate supermarkets, buying in bulk directly from manufacturers and suppliers to offer everyday low prices, then selling directly to consumers — a value-retailing model built on owning (rather than leasing) store real estate and minimising operating costs at every step.

business_core: Dr. Reddy's Laboratories sells generic drugs globally (US, Russia/CIS, India, Europe) alongside a smaller branded and biosimilars business. Revenue leans heavily on the US generics market, where pricing is volatile and one-off patent-cliff opportunities (like its Revlimid/Lenalidomide authorised-generic window) can swing a single product's contribution sharply from year to year.
moat_note: Genuine 'end-to-end' API-to-formulation development capability (a real vertical-integration moat, not just a slogan) + an early, credible biosimilars pipeline in oncology/autoimmune — historically more reliant than Sun Pharma on a handful of high-value US generic opportunities like Lenalidomide, which cuts both ways: outsized windfalls when they land, and now, visible pain as one of those windfalls fades on a schedule set years ago.
name: Dr. Reddy's Laboratories
source_note: Dr. Reddy's own Q3 FY26 SEC Form 6-K filing and earnings call transcript, cross-checked against Investing.com and AlphaSpread
value_chain_note: 10 manufacturing facilities, 7 FDA-approved and 3 EMA-approved — a credible, internationally-inspected manufacturing base, not a single-site concentration risk.
value_chain_position: Integrated generics and emerging biosimilars player — develops products 'end-to-end across API and formulations' per its own FY26 annual report, with historically heavier reliance on a small number of high-value US generic opportunities than Sun Pharma's more diversified, specialty-tilted mix.

business_core: Eicher Motors owns Royal Enfield, India's dominant premium/mid-weight motorcycle brand (350cc-650cc range, sold domestically and increasingly exported to Europe/the Americas), which contributes over 90% of revenue. It also holds roughly half of VE Commercial Vehicles (VECV), a joint venture with Sweden's Volvo Group that makes Eicher-brand trucks and buses — giving it a foothold in commercial vehicles without full standalone exposure to that cycle.
moat_note: Royal Enfield's brand and community moat (heritage, rider culture, global recognition around its 125-year milestone) is close to unique in Indian manufacturing — few domestic brands can charge premium prices purely on brand equity the way Royal Enfield does.
name: Eicher Motors
source_note: Eicher Motors' own Q3 FY26 results (via ICICI Direct and Zee Business), and Business Standard — cross-checked
value_chain_note: Exports remain a small share of Royal Enfield's revenue today but are an explicit long-term lever, with Europe — aligning with the brand's adventure-touring positioning — targeted as a key growth market.
value_chain_position: Eicher's core value-chain position is brand-and-design-led, not scale-led: Royal Enfield competes on heritage, styling and community rather than lowest cost, letting it hold industry-leading margins on relatively modest volumes versus Bajaj or Hero. VECV, by contrast, is a straightforward JV manufacturing play riding India's infrastructure/logistics capex cycle.

as_of: Quarter ended Mar 2026 (SEIL follows an Oct-Sep fiscal year)
business_core: Siemens Energy India designs and supplies power transmission and power generation equipment, including grid technologies and turbines, to utilities and industrial customers. The company was demerged from Siemens Ltd's India operations in March 2025, becoming an independently listed entity focused specifically on the energy equipment business that Siemens Ltd previously housed internally.
moat_note: Siemens global engineering technology and brand trust in power transmission and generation equipment, combined with deep, longstanding utility customer relationships inherited from its time as part of Siemens Ltd's India operations.
name: Siemens Energy India
source_note: Siemens Energy India results coverage (Business Standard, ScanX); Screener.in
value_chain_note: As a demerged entity from Siemens Ltd (also in this dataset), Siemens Energy India's separate listing is itself a real, current structural event — the two companies share a corporate history but now operate, report and trade completely independently, a useful example of how corporate structures in this dataset can change over time.
value_chain_position: Siemens Energy India manufactures and supplies power transmission equipment (grid technologies, transformers) and power generation equipment (turbines) to utility and industrial customers, positioning it as a critical equipment supplier to the same power generation and transmission companies covered elsewhere in this dataset.

as_of: Q4 FY26 (quarter ended 31 Mar 2026); FY26 for full-year figures
business_core: Eternal (renamed from Zomato) is India's leading consumer-internet platform spanning four businesses: Zomato (food delivery), Blinkit (quick commerce — 10-minute grocery/essentials delivery, now the company's biggest growth engine), Hyperpure (B2B restaurant supplies) and District (going-out/events). Because Blinkit shifted from a marketplace model to an inventory-led (1P) model in FY26, reported revenue is mechanically inflated — Net Order Value (NOV, the total value of goods/orders flowing through the platform) is the cleaner metric to judge real business scale, not headline revenue.
moat_note: Genuine network effects and scale in both food delivery (a two-sided marketplace of restaurants and diners built over 18 years) and quick commerce (Blinkit's dark-store density and supply-chain efficiency, now demonstrably profitable). The moat is real and Blinkit's first-ever positive EBITDA is strong evidence it's durable at scale — but quick commerce is a young, capital-intensive, fiercely contested category, so the moat's durability is still being actively tested against well-funded rivals rather than fully established.
name: Eternal (formerly Zomato)
source_note: Eternal's own Q4 FY26 results and shareholder letter, cross-checked against Sahi.com, Business Standard, Angel One and Quash coverage
value_chain_note: The four businesses (Zomato food delivery, Blinkit quick commerce, Hyperpure B2B supplies, District going-out) each sit at different points of maturity — Blinkit is the growth engine, food delivery the steady profitable base, and District/Hyperpure smaller optionality plays still being built.
value_chain_position: Operates asset-heavier than a pure marketplace now — Blinkit's shift to an inventory-led (1P) model and its 2,243 dark stores mean Eternal increasingly owns inventory and last-mile fulfilment infrastructure directly, capturing more margin per order but also carrying more operational and working-capital risk than a commission-only marketplace.

business_core: Federal Bank is a Kerala-headquartered private bank with an unusually large share of India's inward NRI remittances flowing through its accounts, on top of standard retail, SME and corporate lending. It earns the interest spread plus fee income tied to that remittance and treasury franchise.
moat_note: A century-old, deeply entrenched franchise among India's NRI/Kerala diaspora (an unusually sticky, high-quality deposit base) + a newly secured large strategic capital partner (Blackstone) positioning it toward a top-5 private-bank ambition.
name: Federal Bank
source_note: Federal Bank's own Q3 FY26 results and Screener.in's Q3 FY26 ratio summary
value_chain_position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. Federal's distinctive exposure is a century-old NRI and Kerala-diaspora deposit franchise, giving it unusual sensitivity to global-Indian remittance flows and currency movements (Gulf economies, USD/INR).

business_core: GAIL (India) transports natural gas through its pipeline network (Natural Gas Transmission), buys and resells gas to industrial and commercial customers (Natural Gas Marketing), distributes gas to households and vehicles (City Gas), and converts gas into petrochemical products. It is India's largest natural gas transmission and marketing company, majority-owned by the Government of India.
moat_note: An extensive, largely irreplaceable national natural gas pipeline network built over decades with government backing — a genuine infrastructure moat in the regulated transmission business, even though the marketing and petrochemical segments face open commodity competition.
name: GAIL (India)
source_note: GAIL FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (ChartAlert, Univest, PSU Connect); Screener.in
value_chain_note: GAIL's dual nature — part regulated infrastructure utility (transmission, which behaves like a toll road), part commodity trader (marketing and petrochemicals, which behaves like a volatile trading business) — means its overall results can look confusing unless these segments are understood separately, exactly as FY26 demonstrated.
value_chain_position: GAIL sits in the middle of India's natural gas value chain — it transports gas from producers (like ONGC) through its pipeline network, markets and resells gas to industrial customers, distributes gas to city consumers, and converts a portion into petrochemical products, making it both an infrastructure operator and a commodity trader within the same company.

business_core: Godrej Consumer Products manufactures and sells home care products (household insecticides, air fresheners), personal care (soaps) and hair care (colorants) under well-known brands, distributed across India and a significant international footprint including Africa, Indonesia and Latin America. It is one of India's largest home and personal care companies, with genuine geographic diversification beyond the domestic market.
moat_note: Decades of brand trust in Indian home and personal care categories (household insecticides, soaps, hair color), combined with genuine international distribution reach across Africa and other emerging markets that few Indian FMCG peers have replicated.
name: Godrej Consumer Products
source_note: Godrej Consumer Products FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (InvestyWise, ScanX, Univest); Screener.in; Yahoo Finance
value_chain_note: Because a meaningful share of GCPL's business sits outside India, particularly in Africa, its growth story is more exposed to emerging-market currency and demand volatility than domestically-focused peers like Britannia — a real structural difference in risk profile within the same FMCG sector.
value_chain_position: Godrej Consumer Products manufactures and sells home care, personal care and hair care products — household insecticides, soaps, air fresheners and hair colorants — through domestic retail distribution in India and a meaningful international footprint including Africa, positioning it as a branded consumer goods company with genuine geographic diversification beyond most India-focused FMCG peers.

business_core: Grasim Industries is the Aditya Birla Group's diversified holding company. It directly manufactures Viscose Staple Fibre (VSF, a man-made textile fibre) and chlor-alkali/specialty chemicals (which also backward-integrate into VSF production), while separately holding a majority stake (~57%) in UltraTech Cement, a large stake in financial-services group Aditya Birla Capital (NBFC lending, insurance, asset management), and a fast-scaling paints challenger brand, Birla Opus. For investors, owning Grasim means owning a bundle of genuinely different businesses — cement, chemicals, textiles, financial services and paints — through one holding-company structure.
moat_note: Backward-integrated chemicals-to-fibre production gives the core legacy VSF business a genuine cost moat few global peers can match at scale, while majority ownership of India's cement leader (UltraTech) and a rapidly scaling stake in Aditya Birla Capital provide optionality across three separate, large Indian growth markets. The moat here is less about any single product and more about the Aditya Birla Group's demonstrated capital-allocation discipline — funding new bets like paints and B2B e-commerce from cash generated by mature, cash-rich legacy businesses.
name: Grasim Industries
source_note: Grasim's own Q4 & FY26 results release (grasim.com/Aditya Birla Group), cross-checked against Business Standard, Zeebiz and HDFC Sky coverage
sub_sector: Diversified Holding — Cement, Chemicals, Paints, Financial Services
value_chain_note: The Aditya Birla Capital equity raise (Grasim contributing ₹2,880 crore to keep its 52.3% stake) shows the group actively re-investing cash generated by mature businesses (cement, chemicals) into faster-growing ones (financial services, paints) — a deliberate, ongoing capital-allocation strategy, not a one-off.
value_chain_position: Sits at the top of a genuinely diversified group structure — directly running backward-integrated VSF and chlor-alkali chemicals production (the chemicals feed the fibre process itself), while its majority UltraTech stake gives indirect exposure to the full cement value chain and its Aditya Birla Capital stake gives exposure to India's consumer and corporate lending and insurance markets.

business_core: Hindustan Aeronautics designs, manufactures, and maintains fighter aircraft (including the indigenous LCA Tejas), helicopters, aircraft engines, and related systems, selling almost exclusively to the Indian Air Force, Navy and other government defence customers. As India's primary state-owned aerospace manufacturer, its revenue is driven by both new-platform manufacturing and long-term repair, overhaul and maintenance contracts on aircraft already in service.
moat_note: A near-monopoly position as India's primary military aircraft and helicopter manufacturer, built over decades of exclusive government contracts, deep engineering expertise, and a scale of institutional knowledge that no private competitor could replicate without government backing.
name: Hindustan Aeronautics
source_note: HAL FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Drishti earnings call transcript, MarketsMojo); Screener.in; Yahoo Finance
value_chain_note: Because HAL's key inputs — like GE engines for the LCA program — are sourced from foreign suppliers, its delivery timelines are structurally exposed to geopolitical and supply chain factors outside India's control, even though its customer base is almost entirely domestic and government-controlled.
value_chain_position: HAL sits at the top of India's defence aerospace value chain as the primary manufacturer — it designs, builds and maintains fighter aircraft, helicopters, engines and related systems almost exclusively for the Indian armed forces, making it both a supplier and a strategic national-security asset.

business_core: Havells India manufactures and sells electrical products across 24 product verticals spanning cables, consumer durables (via its Lloyd brand of air conditioners, washing machines, refrigerators and TVs), electrical fans and appliances, switchgears, and lighting & fixtures. It is one of India's leading electrical equipment companies, selling to both retail consumers and industrial/infrastructure customers.
moat_note: Decades of brand trust across a diversified electrical products portfolio, from industrial cables to consumer appliances, giving Havells resilience across different demand cycles that narrower single-category competitors lack.
name: Havells India
source_note: Havells India FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (InvestyWise); Screener.in
value_chain_note: Havells' strategic investment in Goldi Solar, a solar equipment maker, gives it indirect exposure to India's renewable energy build-out — a financial link to the same sector already well-represented elsewhere in this dataset (Adani Green, Waaree, Suzlon, NTPC Green), even though Havells itself doesn't manufacture solar products directly.
value_chain_position: Havells manufactures a broad range of electrical products — cables, switchgears, fans, lighting, and consumer appliances under the Lloyd brand — selling through both retail consumer channels and to industrial/infrastructure project customers, giving it exposure to both steady B2B infrastructure demand and more volatile, weather-sensitive consumer demand.

business_core: HCLTech is India's third-largest IT services company, providing software development, IT consulting, engineering R&D services and enterprise software (HCLSoftware) to global clients on the same time-and-materials/project-fee billing model as larger peers TCS and Infosys, with an increasing push into AI-led service offerings as the industry-wide staffing-pyramid growth model faces structural pressure.
moat_note: Scale, decades of enterprise relationships, and a differentiated mix (Engineering & R&D Services plus HCLSoftware, which most peers lack at similar scale) provide some diversification beyond the pure staffing-pyramid model. HCLTech faces the identical AI-transition question as TCS and Infosys — whether scale remains an advantage or becomes a cost disadvantage as AI compresses the economics the industry's pricing model was built around — and this quarter's soft constant-currency growth doesn't yet answer that question either way.
name: HCLTech
source_note: HCLTech's own Q4 & FY26 investor release (hcltech.com), cross-checked against AlphaSpread earnings-call summary and IndMoney coverage
value_chain_note: HCLSoftware's annual recurring revenue actually declined slightly (-0.5% CC) this year and fell sharply in Q4 on seasonality and delayed client decisions — the software diversification arm is not currently a growth engine, worth watching rather than assuming as a given strength.
value_chain_position: A downstream IT service provider like TCS and Infosys, with no physical raw-material chain — competes on delivery quality, cost, and increasingly AI-augmented productivity within client-defined project scope, differentiated by a larger relative weighting toward Engineering & R&D Services (the fastest-growing segment, +9.8% YoY in CC) and its own enterprise software business (HCLSoftware), which most Indian IT peers lack at comparable scale.

business_core: HDFC Bank takes public deposits and lends the money back out across home loans, vehicle loans, credit cards, SME and corporate credit. It earns mainly on the spread between what it pays depositors and what it charges borrowers (net interest margin), plus fees from cards, insurance distribution and transaction banking. It absorbed parent HDFC Ltd, India's largest mortgage lender, in a July 2023 merger.
moat_note: Scale + branch network (9,455+ branches) + brand trust. India's largest private bank by assets and one of three systemically important banks.
name: HDFC Bank
source_note: HDFC Bank Q3 FY26 results filing (SEC Form 6-K) and Q4 FY26 investor presentation
value_chain_note: BIS research on UPI confirms this directly: banks hold a small share of the UPI transaction-initiation market by value, even as the dominant non-bank apps (Google Pay, PhonePe) capture the customer relationship at the point of use.
value_chain_position: No physical value chain — banks face a platform disintermediation dynamic instead. HDFC Bank holds the customer's account and the underlying money, but the customer-facing 'app layer' for everyday payments increasingly belongs to someone else.

as_of: FY26 (year ended 31 Mar 2026); embedded value/RoEV as of 9M FY26 (most recent disclosed)
business_core: HDFC Life (part of the HDFC Bank group) is one of India's largest private life insurers, selling protection, savings, pension, ULIP and annuity products through a genuinely diversified mix of bancassurance, agency and digital/direct channels — a less single-bank-concentrated distribution model than SBI Life's SBI-branch-led approach. Like all life insurers, economics are driven by APE (new-business volume), VNB margin (profitability of that new business), and Embedded Value (the actuarial measure of total company worth).
moat_note: HDFC Bank's brand and distribution reach, combined with a genuinely diversified multi-channel distribution strategy and a long-standing reputation for claims-servicing quality (99.8% settlement ratio), form a real trust-and-distribution moat. It's a moat that protects market position more than it currently insulates near-term profitability or growth pace — both are visibly trailing SBI Life's on the metrics available this year.
name: HDFC Life Insurance
source_note: HDFC Life's own FY26 press release (hdfclife.com) and ICICI Direct Research 9M FY26 result update, cross-checked against APAC Media News coverage
value_chain_note: That channel diversification is a double-edged trait: less single-point dependency risk than SBI Life, but also no single channel gives HDFC Life quite the same overwhelming distribution scale advantage SBI Life gets from SBI's branch network.
value_chain_position: Sits at the underwriting/manufacturing end of the insurance value chain — designing and pricing policies and bearing actuarial/investment risk — with distribution spread across bancassurance (HDFC Bank), agency and digital/direct channels rather than concentrated in one dominant bank relationship, a structurally more diversified model than SBI Life's.

business_core: Hindalco is the metals flagship of the Aditya Birla Group — India's largest aluminium producer and a significant copper smelter/refiner domestically, plus (via its wholly-owned subsidiary Novelis) the world's largest aluminium rolling and recycling company, supplying can sheet and auto-body sheet to global beverage and automotive customers. Roughly 60-65% of consolidated revenue comes from Novelis, so the stock's fortunes are genuinely a blend of a fast-growing India metals story and a much larger, more industrial-cycle-sensitive global rolling business.
moat_note: Full value-chain integration — from bauxite and alumina through primary metal smelting to Novelis's global #1 position in aluminium rolling and recycling — is a scale-and-technology moat few competitors can match end-to-end. The flip side: Novelis's international manufacturing footprint imports risks (plant fires, tariffs, global industrial-demand cycles) that a purely domestic metals producer simply wouldn't be exposed to.
name: Hindalco Industries
source_note: Hindalco's own Q4 & FY26 results press release (hindalco.com / Aditya Birla Group), cross-checked against Business Standard, Groww and AlCircle
value_chain_note: The India business and Novelis are genuinely different investments bundled into one stock — a domestic, high-margin, high-growth metals franchise, and a larger, lower-margin, more industrially cyclical global rolling business with its own separate risks.
value_chain_position: One of the most vertically integrated metals businesses on the exchange — spanning domestic bauxite mining and alumina refining, primary aluminium and copper smelting in India, and (via Novelis) advanced downstream rolling and the world's largest aluminium recycling operation feeding directly into global beverage-can and automotive-body-panel supply chains.

business_core: Hindustan Unilever (Unilever's Indian arm) is India's largest FMCG company: it manufactures and sells everyday packaged goods — soaps, detergents, shampoos, packaged foods, skincare — under brands like Lux, Surf Excel and Dove, distributed through India's largest retail reach network. Margins depend on brand pricing power versus input costs (palm oil and other commodity derivatives).
moat_note: 20 'billion-rupee brands' including Dove, Surf Excel, Lux, Lipton, Horlicks, and Lakmé — a brand portfolio moat paired with a 9-million-outlet distribution network built over 90+ years. The moat is real; the question is whether the premium valuation already prices it in fully.
name: Hindustan Unilever
source_note: HUL's own Q3 FY26 earnings call (Feb 12 2026), Business Standard, Axis Direct research note, Screener.in (FY26 annual), MarketsMojo — six sources cross-checked
value_chain_note: Import-dependent on palm oil and crude-linked raw materials, named directly as the Q3 gross-margin headwind — a live, current example of the import-exposure pattern, not a hypothetical.
value_chain_position: Downstream brand owner and manufacturer — HUL doesn't grow its own raw materials, it captures value through 20 'billion-rupee' brands and a 9-million-outlet distribution network that turns commodity inputs into premium-priced finished goods.

business_core: Hindustan Zinc mines and refines zinc, lead and silver from integrated operations in Rajasthan, selling refined metal domestically and internationally. It is the world's largest integrated zinc producer and among the top global silver producers, operating as a majority-owned subsidiary of Vedanta Limited, with the Government of India holding a significant minority stake.
moat_note: World's largest integrated zinc producer with a best-in-class, low-cost operating structure and decades of mining rights across Rajasthan's Zawar, Rajpura Dariba, Kayad, Rampura Agucha and Sindesar Khurd mines — scale and resource access that would take a competitor decades to replicate.
name: Hindustan Zinc
source_note: Hindustan Zinc FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Sahi, Univest, MarketsMojo, Upstox); Screener.in
value_chain_note: As a majority-owned subsidiary of Vedanta Limited, Hindustan Zinc's capital allocation — including how much cash gets paid out as dividends versus reinvested — is significantly influenced by its parent's own financial needs, a structural governance dynamic that doesn't apply to standalone metals companies.
value_chain_position: Hindustan Zinc mines zinc, lead and silver ore, then smelts and refines it into finished metal, selling into steel galvanisation (zinc's primary end-use), battery and industrial applications (lead), and jewellery, electronics and solar/EV manufacturing (silver) — an integrated upstream-to-refined-metal producer rather than a pure miner.

business_core: Hyundai Motor India designs, manufactures and sells passenger vehicles — including the Venue, Verna, Exter, Creta and Aura — for both the domestic Indian market and export to emerging markets, as a wholly-owned subsidiary of South Korea's Hyundai Motor Company. It is India's second-largest passenger vehicle manufacturer and exporter.
moat_note: Three decades of brand trust in India, a wide dealership and service network, and backing from Hyundai Motor Company's global platform engineering and design — advantages a new entrant would need many years and enormous capital to replicate.
name: Hyundai Motor India
source_note: Hyundai Motor India FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (company press release, MarketsMojo, ScanX, InvestyWise); Screener.in; Kotak Neo
value_chain_note: As a wholly-owned Korean subsidiary, Hyundai India's capacity decisions (like the new Pune plant) and product roadmap are made with both the Indian market and Hyundai's global export hub strategy in mind, meaning its growth isn't purely a bet on domestic demand alone.
value_chain_position: Hyundai Motor India designs, manufactures and sells passenger vehicles domestically and for export, sourcing components from a large supplier base (including companies like Bosch and Motherson elsewhere in this dataset) and selling through dealership networks — positioning it squarely as an OEM at the consumer-facing end of the automotive value chain.

business_core: ICICI Bank runs the same core model as any large bank — deposits fund a loan book spanning retail mortgages, vehicle loans, SME and corporate credit — and profits from the interest spread plus fee income from cards, wealth management and insurance cross-sold through its digital apps (iMobile, InstaBIZ).
moat_note: Brand trust + scale + a deep retail/digital franchise. India's second-largest private bank, consistently gaining share in retail and MSME lending.
name: ICICI Bank
source_note: ICICI Bank's own Q3 FY26 performance review (icicibank.com) and Screener.in's Q3 FY26 ratio summary
value_chain_note: A well-diversified retail and corporate book — no single-segment concentration flagged.
value_chain_position: No physical value chain — like all banks it faces platform disintermediation: it holds the customer account and the money, while the everyday-payments app layer increasingly belongs to third-party UPI apps. ICICI has answered with one of the strongest in-house digital franchises (iMobile, InstaBIZ) among private banks.

business_core: ICICI Lombard General Insurance sells non-life insurance products — including motor, health, property and liability coverage — to individuals and businesses, collecting premiums and paying out claims, while investing the pooled premium float for additional income. It is one of India's largest private-sector general insurers, jointly promoted by ICICI Bank and Canada's Fairfax Financial Holdings.
moat_note: Backing from ICICI Bank's brand and distribution network, combined with decades of underwriting data and a diversified product portfolio spanning health, motor and commercial lines, giving ICICI Lombard scale advantages that smaller general insurers cannot easily match.
name: ICICI Lombard General Insurance
source_note: ICICI Lombard FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Sahi, ICICI Direct); Screener.in; Kotak Neo
value_chain_note: General insurance differs structurally from life insurance in that policies are typically annual and claims can arise quickly (a car accident, a hospital visit), making underwriting discipline and claims management far more central to profitability quarter-to-quarter than in the multi-decade life insurance business.
value_chain_position: ICICI Lombard collects insurance premiums from individuals and businesses across health, motor, property and liability lines, invests that pooled capital, and pays out claims when policyholders experience covered losses — a classic general (non-life) insurance model, distinct from LIC's life insurance business elsewhere in this dataset.

business_core: IDFC First Bank is a young private bank (formed from the 2018 merger of IDFC Bank and Capital First) that built a mostly retail loan book from scratch — home loans, vehicle loans, MSME credit and a fast-growing credit-card business — while still working through legacy corporate/infrastructure-project loans inherited from IDFC's earlier project-finance roots.
moat_note: A purpose-built, fintech-grade digital banking experience (built fresh after the 2018 IDFC Bank–Capital First merger, not retrofitted onto a legacy core) + the fastest CASA-funded growth among private banks tracked here — still a relatively young, unproven-through-a-full-credit-cycle franchise compared to century-old peers.
name: IDFC First Bank
source_note: IDFC First Bank's own Q3 FY26 press release, investor presentation, and earnings-call transcript
value_chain_position: No physical chain; the shared platform-disintermediation dynamic. IDFC First's specific exposure is a meaningful microfinance and unsecured book at a time of sector-wide MFI stress — even as its purpose-built digital stack and high CASA give it a strong funding base to weather it.

business_core: The Indian Hotels Company (IHCL) owns, operates and manages hotels under the Taj, Vivanta, SeleQtions and Ginger brands across India and internationally, alongside adjacent hospitality businesses including F&B delivery (Qmin), boutique home stays (amã Stays & Trails) and resorts (Tree of Life). It is India's largest and most storied hospitality company, part of the Tata Group, with an accelerating shift toward asset-light, management-fee-based growth.
moat_note: The Taj brand's century-plus reputation for luxury Indian hospitality, combined with an accelerating asset-light management-contract growth model that extends the brand's reach without proportional capital investment — a durable, increasingly capital-efficient moat.
name: Indian Hotels Company (IHCL)
source_note: Indian Hotels Company (IHCL) FY26 & Q4 FY26 results coverage (Hotelier India, MarketsMojo); Screener.in
value_chain_note: IHCL's growing management-fee income (+22% in FY26) reflects a deliberate shift toward an asset-light growth model — expanding its brand footprint into hotels it manages but doesn't own — a structurally different, higher-return growth path than the capital-intensive owned-hotel model that built the Taj brand originally.
value_chain_position: Indian Hotels Company (IHCL) owns, operates and manages hotels under the Taj, Vivanta, SeleQtions and Ginger brands, plus adjacent hospitality businesses (F&B delivery via Qmin, boutique stays via amã, resorts via Tree of Life), earning revenue from room bookings, food & beverage, and management fees on hotels it operates but doesn't own — positioning it at the direct consumer-facing end of India's hospitality and tourism value chain.

as_of: FY26 (year ended 31 Mar 2026); Q4 FY26 for capacity
business_core: IndiGo (InterGlobe Aviation) is India's largest airline by market share, operating 441 aircraft across 97 domestic and 45 international destinations on a low-cost-carrier model. Revenue comes from passenger ticket sales plus a growing ancillary business (baggage, seat selection, cargo), while costs are dominated by fuel and aircraft-lease expenses — unit economics (revenue and cost per available-seat-kilometre) matter more than absolute revenue for judging underlying airline profitability.
moat_note: India's largest airline by domestic market share and route network breadth, reinforced by strong balance-sheet liquidity that lets it weather industry downturns better than smaller or more leveraged rivals — a real advantage in a historically brutal, bankruptcy-prone industry.
